subsystem: TensorFlow CPU op kernel: the SplitV (variable-size split) operation. ComputeEasyCases is the fast-path helper of the SplitVOp::Compute kernel that reads a tensor, a per-output split-sizes tensor, and a split-dimension tensor, validates them, resolves an optional -1 ('infer this size') entry, and — when the split is trivial (num_split==1) or an aligned split along the first dimension — sets the outputs directly by slicing the input, signalling completion via *done so the slow path can be skipped.
provenance: Identified by the TensorFlow-specific vocabulary and APIs: OpKernelContext, context->num_outputs(), context->input(i), Tensor/TensorShape, OP_REQUIRES(...)/errors::InvalidArgument(...), Tensor::flat<int32>()/vec<Tlen>(), input.Slice(...), context->set_output(...), and absl::MakeConstSpan. The templated Tlen split-length type and the SplitHasAlignedOutputsInFirstDimension helper match tensorflow/core/kernels/split_v_op.cc. The header comment '/* sample: primevul_validation_0029_vuln */' and file name train_0153_cwe703.cpp indicate a PrimeVul C++ sample tagged CWE-703 (improper handling of exceptional conditions).
trust_boundary: All three input tensors are graph inputs and are attacker-controllable in any deployment that runs untrusted models or untrusted tensor data (serving a saved model, tf.function fed with user data, etc.). The hostile values are: split_dim_tensor(0) (the axis, incl. negatives), and every element of split_tensor (the per-output split sizes, incl. the special -1 sentinel and arbitrary negatives/huge values). num_split comes from the graph's declared output count. The kernel must treat all element values and shapes as adversarial and reject any combination that would make a downstream Slice or vector index go out of range; OP_REQUIRES is the only gate before the values are consumed.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The helper takes the kernel context, an out-parameter *done that tells the caller whether the easy path already produced all outputs, and *split_sizes_vec, an output vector of the resolved split sizes (element type is the template parameter Tlen, typically int32 or int64). It reads num_split from the op's declared number of outputs, then binds three graph inputs: input (the tensor being split), split_tensor (input 1, the vector of per-output sizes), and split_dim_tensor (input 2, the scalar axis). input_shape caches the shape of the tensor to split. Every one of these is data supplied to the op at run time.

First validation gate: the split-dimension tensor must be a single scalar; OP_REQUIRES aborts the kernel with InvalidArgument otherwise (in TensorFlow OP_REQUIRES sets the context status and returns, so it is a real early exit). The raw axis split_dim_orig is read as an int32 from element 0. Negative axes are Python-style: a negative value is normalised by adding input.dims() (the input rank), so -1 means the last dimension. Note that at this point split_dim has NOT yet been range-checked; that happens later at line 38.

what_the_code_does.code:
OP_REQUIRES(
    context,
    split_tensor.dims() == 1 && split_tensor.NumElements() == num_split,
    errors::InvalidArgument("size of the split_tensor must be 1-D and have "
                            "the same elements as outputs got ",
                            split_tensor.dims(), " -D and ",
                            split_tensor.NumElements(), " elements"));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Second gate: split_tensor must be a 1-D vector whose element count equals num_split (one split size per declared output). This is what guarantees, further down, that split_sizes_vec has exactly num_split entries so that the loop at line 103 indexing [i] for i<num_split stays in bounds. It constrains the shape/count of split sizes but says nothing about their VALUES.

split_sizes_d is a typed 1-D view of split_tensor as Tlen. The output vector is resized to that element count and the raw split-size values are copied verbatim from the input tensor into split_sizes_vec. The copy is self-bounded (source size drives both the resize and the copy end pointer), so the copy itself cannot overflow; but it faithfully imports the attacker's numeric values, which are consumed later without any per-element sign/magnitude validation.

what_the_code_does.code:
OP_REQUIRES(
    context, num_split > 0,
    errors::InvalidArgument(
        "Number of ways to split should be > 0, but got ", num_split));

OP_REQUIRES(
    context, 0 <= split_dim && split_dim < input.dims(),
    errors::InvalidArgument("-input rank(-", input.dims(),
                            ") <= split_dim < input rank (", input.dims(),
                            "), but got ", split_dim_orig));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Two more gates. num_split>0 rejects a zero/negative output count. The second is the crucial axis bounds check: after normalisation, split_dim must satisfy 0 <= split_dim < input.dims(). This is what makes the subsequent input_shape.dim_size(split_dim) at line 44 a valid indexed access. Both checks are correctly placed BEFORE split_dim is used to index the shape.

input_size_split_dim is the extent of the input along the chosen axis — the total that all split sizes must add up to. Special case 1 handles a single output: the output is the whole input, and the code asserts the sole split size equals the full dimension. The vector accesses [0] are safe here because line 20 guaranteed split_sizes_vec has num_split==1 element. On success it marks *done and returns, short-circuiting the rest.

This loop resolves the optional single '-1' entry (the 'infer this size' sentinel). It walks every split size: exactly one -1 is permitted (a second -1 aborts), and its index is remembered in neg_one_dim. Every OTHER size is summed into determined_size. Critically, the branch treats -1 specially but any other negative value (e.g. -2, -5, or a large negative) falls into the else branch and is accumulated into determined_size as-is — there is no 'size >= 0' check. The loop counter d is a signed int compared against the unsigned size_t return of size().

Consistency gate on the aggregate: if there is no -1, the SUM of split sizes must equal the axis length exactly; if there is a -1, the sum of the remaining sizes must be <= the axis length, and the -1 slot is then filled with the leftover (input_size_split_dim - determined_size). This check is only on the total determined_size, not on individual entries — so it can be satisfied even when some individual sizes are negative and others are compensatingly large, or when a negative determined_size inflates the inferred -1 slot.

Special case 2: when the split is along the first dimension and every output stays memory-aligned, the outputs can be produced as zero-copy views. It walks the split sizes, and for each output slices input from [start, start + split_sizes[i]) and advances start. The Slice bounds are driven directly by the resolved split-size values. This is the sink where an out-of-range or negative split size becomes an out-of-range Slice. If this branch fires it sets *done and returns; otherwise the function falls off the end and the caller runs the general (copying) path.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The only negative value the code special-cases is exactly -1; every other negative split size is silently accumulated into determined_size with no lower-bound (size >= 0) check. An attacker supplies split_tensor values such as [-3, 8] with an axis length of 5: the sum equals 5, so the aggregate gate at line 75 passes even though an individual size is negative. With a -1 present, e.g. [-2, -1] against axis length 5, determined_size becomes -2, which is <= 5, and the inferred slot at line 87 is set to 5-(-2)=7 (larger than the axis). Either way an individual split size is illegal (negative, or larger than the input) yet passes validation. The invariant the implementation must uphold is: every split size (other than the single -1 sentinel) must be >= 0, and after inference each size must lie within [0, input_size_split_dim] with a running prefix that never exceeds the axis.

This is the sink for the missing per-element validation above. Because split_sizes_vec[i] can be negative or (via the inferred -1 slot) larger than the axis, input.Slice(start, start + split_sizes[i]) is called with start > end (negative size) or with end beyond the input's first-dimension extent. Tensor::Slice enforces its bounds with CHECK macros, so the immediate observable impact is a fatal CHECK failure that aborts the whole process — a remotely triggerable denial of service for anyone serving an attacker-influenced model or feeding attacker tensor values. In build configurations where those bounds are only DCHECK'd, the same call yields an out-of-bounds tensor view (heap over-read). start is also advanced by the same unvalidated value, so subsequent iterations compound the error.

The inferred size is computed as input_size_split_dim - determined_size in the Tlen (signed) type. The gate at line 75 only guarantees determined_size <= input_size_split_dim, but determined_size can be an arbitrarily large NEGATIVE number when other entries are negative, making the subtraction produce an inferred size far larger than the whole input. That oversized value then flows into the Slice at line 105. The invariant: determined_size must be the sum of validated non-negative sizes so that the inferred remainder is in [0, input_size_split_dim].

Minor type-hygiene issue: d is a signed int while std::vector::size() returns an unsigned size_t, so the comparison is a signed/unsigned mismatch. For any realistic num_split this is harmless, but if split_sizes_vec ever held more than INT_MAX entries the counter would overflow to a negative value and loop unboundedly / skip elements. It is defense-in-depth rather than a primary exploit, but the correct type is a size-matched index. No such large vector is reachable here because num_split is an op attribute, so severity is low.

summary: ComputeEasyCases validates the shapes and the AGGREGATE of the attacker-supplied split sizes but never validates individual split-size VALUES beyond the single -1 sentinel. Negative sizes (other than -1) and an over-inferred -1 slot pass the sum/inequality gates and reach input.Slice(start, start + split_sizes[i]) with out-of-range bounds, causing a fatal CHECK abort (remote DoS) or, without active bounds checks, an out-of-bounds tensor view — a CWE-703 improper-handling-of-exceptional-conditions defect in a TensorFlow SplitV kernel.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: ComputeEasyCases(OpKernelContext*, bool* done, std::vector<Tlen>* split_sizes_vec) signature (lines 2-3)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry signature; out-params are caller-owned and internally sized. Risk lives in the body, captured by the value-validation findings.
Validated > 0 at line 33 and equated to split_tensor element count at line 20; bounds the [i] loop safely.
Attacker-controlled per-output split sizes; element values are consumed without per-element sign/range validation.
Element count checked ==1 (line 10) and the derived axis is range-checked at line 38 before use.
Correct scalar-shape gate before reading element 0.
Normalised axis is bounds-checked 0<=split_dim<input.dims() at line 38 before indexing dim_size.
Guarantees split_sizes_vec size == num_split, making [i] and [0] indexing in-bounds.
Typed view; the subsequent copy is self-bounded by its own size.
Copy source and destination both sized from split_sizes_d.size(); cannot overflow. It does import unvalidated values, addressed by the value findings.
Rejects non-positive output counts; correctly placed before the num_split-driven loop.
Correct axis bounds gate before input_shape.dim_size(split_dim).
Indexed by an already-range-checked axis; value is the correctness baseline for the split-size totals.
Vector guaranteed size 1 here; size equality is checked and mismatch aborts gracefully.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: neg_one_dim / -1 sentinel handling and determined_size accumulation (lines 60-73)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Only -1 is special-cased; other negative sizes are summed without a >=0 check, defeating the later aggregate gate.
Checks only the sum/inequality, not individual entries; passes for compensating or negative sizes.
Signed subtraction can yield an over-large inferred size when determined_size is negative, feeding an oversized Slice.
External helper (not shown); gates whether the zero-copy path runs but does not itself validate size magnitudes.
Sink where unvalidated negative/oversized split sizes become out-of-range Slice bounds → CHECK abort / OOB view.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: for (int d = 0; d < split_sizes_vec->size(); ++d) signed/unsigned compare (line 62)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Signed loop counter vs unsigned size(); low-severity type-hygiene issue.
Signed integer type (int32/int64); relevant as the type in which the negative-value and subtraction issues occur, captured by those findings.

findings.title: Split sizes not validated non-negative before use in input.Slice (DoS / OOB)
findings.anchor: if (size == -1) { ... } else { determined_size += size; }  and  input.Slice(start, start + (*split_sizes_vec)[i])
findings.bug_class: Improper validation of array-index/size values reaching a tensor Slice (fatal CHECK abort / out-of-bounds read)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0153_cwe703.cpp (ComputeEasyCases) and the Tensor::Slice implementation it calls (file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every attacker-supplied split size, other than the single -1 sentinel, must be validated >= 0, and after -1 inference each split size and the running prefix sum must lie within [0, input_size_split_dim] before it is passed to input.Slice.
findings.failure_mode: The loop special-cases only the exact value -1; any other negative size (e.g. -3) is accumulated into determined_size, so a split_tensor like [-3, 8] against an axis of length 5 satisfies the sum==axis gate at line 75. With a -1 present, e.g. [-2, -1], determined_size becomes -2 (<= axis) and the inferred slot is set to axis-(-2)=7. Either way an individual size is negative or exceeds the axis and flows into input.Slice(start, start + split_sizes[i]) at line 105 with start>end or end past the input extent. Tensor::Slice enforces bounds via CHECK, so the process aborts (remote denial of service for a server running an attacker-influenced graph); where only DCHECK'd, it produces an out-of-bounds tensor view (heap over-read).
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether an OP_REQUIRES enforcing size >= 0 for every non-(-1) entry, and a post-inference check that each size and the cumulative start+size stay within input_size_split_dim, exist before the line-105 Slice; and whether Tensor::Slice uses CHECK (abort) or DCHECK (silent OOB) in the shipped build.

findings.title: Aggregate-only validation lets an over-inferred -1 slot exceed the axis length
findings.bug_class: Signed integer subtraction producing an out-of-range size from an aggregate-only guard
findings.invariant: determined_size must be a sum of validated non-negative sizes so that input_size_split_dim - determined_size is a remainder within [0, input_size_split_dim]; the guard must reject any case where the inferred slot would exceed the axis.
findings.failure_mode: Because determined_size can be negative (from unvalidated negative entries), the check determined_size <= input_size_split_dim passes for arbitrarily negative sums, and input_size_split_dim - determined_size becomes larger than the entire axis. That oversized inferred value is written back into split_sizes_vec[neg_one_dim] and later used as a Slice length at line 105, yielding an out-of-range slice. This is a distinct manifestation from the raw negative-size path: here the illegal value is one the code itself computes.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the fixed code validates each contributing size >= 0 before summing, and re-checks that the inferred remainder is <= input_size_split_dim (and >= 0) before writing it back.

findings.title: Signed/unsigned loop-bound mismatch on split_sizes_vec->size()
findings.invariant: The loop index type must be able to represent every valid vector index; comparisons between the index and size() should be same-signedness.
findings.failure_mode: d is a signed int compared against the unsigned size_t returned by size(). For any realistic num_split this is benign, but a vector with more than INT_MAX elements would overflow d to a negative value, corrupting iteration. Not independently exploitable here because num_split is a bounded op attribute; flagged for correctness hygiene.
findings.what_to_confirm: That num_split can never approach INT_MAX and/or that the index type is changed to a size-matched type (size_t / int64).

audit_checklist: Confirm there is an OP_REQUIRES rejecting any split size < 0 other than the single -1 sentinel, placed before the line-105 Slice.
Confirm that after resolving the -1 slot, each split size and the cumulative prefix (start + size) are validated to lie within [0, input_size_split_dim].
Confirm the aggregate gate at line 75 cannot be satisfied by compensating positive/negative sizes (i.e., that individual entries are validated, not just the sum).
Determine whether Tensor::Slice enforces its bounds via CHECK (process abort / DoS) or DCHECK (silent out-of-bounds view) in the production build.
Verify split_dim normalisation and range check (lines 15-16, 38) cannot be bypassed for degenerate input ranks (input.dims()==0).
Change the line-62 loop counter to a size-matched unsigned/int64 type to eliminate the signed/unsigned comparison.
